title: Discipleship, Part 2: Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 2 - Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Review of Part 1 Definitions and the Word 'Disciple'
subpoints: Five definitions of discipleship reviewed, from broad to narrow
Two constants across all definitions: discipleship is always aimed at Christ-likeness and always centered on the Bible
Every Christian is a disciple -- Acts 11:26 uses 'disciples' and 'Christians' interchangeably
Luke 14 marks: loves Christ supremely, bears the cross daily, forsakes all

outline.title: 2. Passing Truth Across Generations: Psalm 78 and 2 Timothy 2
subpoints: Psalm 78:1-8 involves four generations of passing on 'the praises of the LORD and his wonderful works'
Judges 2:10 -- the saddest verse: one generation did not tell the next, and they did not know God
2 Timothy 2:1-2 -- Paul to Timothy to faithful men to others: the chain of transmission from person to person
Discipleship is intrinsically generational and relational -- passing on the word of God

outline.title: 3. What Does Discipleship Involve? (Six Elements)
subpoints: Learning and loving God's word (John 8:31-32): abiding in the word marks a true disciple
Pursuing peace and edifying one another (Romans 14:19)
Bearing with one another (Colossians 3:12-13)
Encouraging holy living and helping each other see blind spots (Hebrews 3:12-13)
Lighting a fire for love and good works -- stirring each other up (Hebrews 10:24)
The goal according to Ephesians 4:13: unity of faith, maturity, and fullness of Christ

outline.title: 4. Why Discipleship Matters
subpoints: God says so -- and obedience to God, when we get it right, brings joy we miss when we refuse
Discipleship is the means God uses to preserve us until Jesus returns (Hebrews 10:23-26)
The stakes are very high: those not involved in this process may fall away from Christ
God loves us and discipleship is his way of moving us further from the ways of the world and closer to himself

outline.title: 5. How Should We Disciple One Another? (Overview)
subpoints: Discipleship only happens where people *want* to be discipled -- we must also think about where we need discipling
Two pillars: the Lord's Day with public worship and relationships; and doing life together
Speaking the truth in love (Ephesians 4:15) -- the truth is to be used to grow, never to hurt
Every member doing his or her part (Ephesians 4:16) -- no bench players in the church of Jesus Christ

summary:
This second installment continues the Discipleship series with a 30,000-foot overview of what discipleship involves, why it matters, and how it should happen -- all developed from Ephesians 4:11-16. Pastor Tito Lyro reviews the five definitions of discipleship from the previous lesson, identifies two constants (discipleship always aims at Christ-likeness and always centers on the Bible), and re-establishes that every Christian is a disciple and a disciple-maker simultaneously.

The lesson introduces the essential intergenerational dimension of discipleship through Psalm 78:1-8, which involves four generations of passing on the praises of the Lord. The haunting verse of Judges 2:10 -- where one generation failed to pass on what they knew, and the next generation did not know God -- shows the stakes. Second Timothy 2:1-2 then demonstrates the personal chain of transmission: Paul to Timothy to faithful men to others still.

The lesson surveys six elements of what discipleship involves, grounding each in a specific Scripture: learning and loving God's word (John 8:31-32); pursuing peace and edifying one another (Romans 14:19); bearing with one another (Colossians 3:12-13); encouraging holy living (Hebrews 3:12-13); lighting a fire for love and good works (Hebrews 10:24); and the goal of unity of faith, maturity, and the fullness of Christ (Ephesians 4:13).

The lesson addresses why discipleship matters with a particular focus on Hebrews 10:23-26: discipleship is the means God uses to preserve us until the return of Jesus Christ. Finally, the beginning of how-to is raised: discipleship only happens where people want it, we must think about where we ourselves need discipling, and the two pillars are the Lord's Day and doing life together. The lesson closes with Ephesians 4:15-16 -- speaking the truth in love and every member doing his or her part.
transcript:
## Discipleship, Part 2: Growing Together as Disciples of Jesus

*Pastor Tito Lyro -- Sunday School*

---

As we begin, we return to Ephesians chapter 4. We're continuing our series on discipleship. This is our second lesson. If you missed the first one, I encourage you to go to our website and listen to it or sign up for our podcast.

The concept I want to first consider today is that we have to **grow together** as disciples of Jesus. Today we're going to cover a lot of things in a 30,000-foot overview that we'll develop as we go on with our series.

Look at Ephesians 4:11-16:

> *And he himself gave some to be apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, and some pastors and teachers, for the equipping of the saints for the work of ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ, till we all come to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to a perfect man, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ; that we should no longer be children, tossed to and fro and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the trickery of men, in the cunning craftiness of deceitful plotting, but, speaking the truth in love, may grow up in all things into him who is the head -- Christ -- from whom the whole body, joined and knit together by what every joint supplies, according to the effective working by which every part does its share, causes growth of the body for the edifying of itself in love.*

Here we have, in summary, the entire life of the church and the entire life of Christians in the church.

### Review of Part 1

In our first lesson we considered the word *disciple*: a learner who learns from a master, both by what the master teaches and by what the master does. The disciple is in a close bond with the one teaching him and exists in fellowship with other disciples of the same master.

We considered four definitions of discipleship:
1. Doing life together as people who have been redeemed by Jesus and are following him every moment
2. Teaching biblical precepts while modeling and guiding others toward living righteously as followers of Jesus Christ
3. The intentional encouragement and training of disciples of Jesus on the basis of deliberate, loving relationships
4. The constant process in which a Christian is helped by the covenant community to grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ so that he becomes progressively conformed to the image of Jesus Christ and can in turn disciple others

A fifth: **Discipleship is the mutual helping of one another in the Church of Jesus Christ, in order to become more like the one who saved us and whom we follow.** Note the addition of the word *mutual*: the other definitions implied one direction, but the Bible teaches it goes both ways. Everybody is a disciple; everybody should also be discipling.

Two constants in all these definitions: (1) discipleship is always aimed at becoming more like Jesus Christ, and (2) discipleship is always centered on the Bible. Fun activities together are not discipleship. Discipleship has to be around the Bible.

**Every Christian is a disciple.** Acts 11:26 -- disciples were first called Christians in Antioch. The words are interchangeable. You don't become a Christian and then later become a disciple. Every Christian is also a disciple who makes disciples.

### Passing Truth Across Generations

Before we answer "How should we disciple one another?" let's look at Psalm 78:1-8:

> *Give ear, O my people, to my law; incline your ears to the words of my mouth. I will open my mouth in a parable; I will utter dark sayings of old, which we have heard and known, and our fathers have told us. We will not hide them from their children, telling to the generation to come the praises of the LORD, and his strength and his wonderful works that he has done... that the generation to come might know them, the children who would be born, that they may arise and declare them to their children, that they may set their hope in God.*

How many generations are involved? Four. Fathers, to us, to the generation to come, to their children. The passing of truth is intrinsic to discipleship. If one generation doesn't do the job of passing it on, the truth ends -- at least for that group.

We see this in Judges 2:10 -- one of the saddest verses in the whole Bible: the previous generation did not tell them about the works of God, and *that generation did not know God.* Just one generation removed from those who saw the pillar of fire in the wilderness.

And 2 Timothy 2:1-2 -- Paul's last letter, possibly written within weeks of his beheading:

> *You therefore, my son, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus. And the things that you have heard from me among many witnesses, commit these to faithful men who will be able to teach others also.*

Paul -- Timothy -- faithful men -- others. The chain of transmission. Not just generational but personal: from one person to the next.

Colossians 3:16 says the word of Christ dwells in us richly when we are sharing it with others. If you're not sharing the word with your brothers and sisters in wisdom, then the word of Christ doesn't really dwell in you -- you are a black hole of God's word.

### What Does Discipleship Involve?

**Learning and loving God's word.** John 8:31-32: *"If you abide in my word, you are my disciples indeed."* Discipleship involves learning and loving God's word.

**Pursuing peace and edifying one another.** Romans 14:19: *"Let us pursue the things which make for peace and the things by which one may edify another."*

**Bearing with one another.** Colossians 3:12-13: *"Put on tender mercies, kindness, humility, meekness, long-suffering, bearing with one another, and forgiving one another."*

**Encouraging holy living.** Hebrews 3:12-13: *"Beware, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God; but exhort one another daily, while it is called 'Today,' lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin."* We have blind spots that we cannot see -- we need others to help us walk in holiness.

**Lighting a fire for love and good works.** Hebrews 10:24: *"Let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works."* The word "stir up" is often used in extra-biblical literature of a sibling poking another sibling -- not to cause trouble, but to stir each other up to love and good works.

### The Goal of Discipleship: Ephesians 4:13

*"...till we all come to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to a perfect man, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ."*

Three goals:
1. **Unity** of the faith and knowledge of the Son of God -- discipleship should cause us to be more united
2. **Maturity** -- the "perfect man" is the mature man. An immature Christian is what the Bible calls a "weaker brother" -- not a term of honor
3. **Fullness** -- growing into all that Christ has for us, being transformed from glory to glory into his image

Discipleship and sanctification go hand in hand; they are describing the same process.

### Why Does Discipleship Matter?

God says so -- that's the primary reason, even if it feels unsatisfying. When we obey God and get it right, there is a joy we miss when we refuse.

But also: **discipleship is the means God uses to preserve us until Jesus returns.** Hebrews 10:23-25:

> *Let us hold fast the confession of our hope without wavering... and let us consider one another in order to stir up love and good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is the manner of some, but exhorting one another, and so much the more as you see the Day approaching.*

The stakes are very high. Hebrews 10:26 and following describe what happens to those who fall away from this process. The process of discipleship is what God has appointed to get us to the end by his grace.

### How Should We Disciple One Another?

Discipleship only happens where people *want* to be discipled. But we often think only about how we can disciple others, and rarely about where we need discipling ourselves. Since discipleship is mutual, we need to ask both questions.

**Two pillars of discipleship:** (1) The Lord's Day with public and private worship and relationships, and (2) doing life together.

Hebrews 10:24-25 brings these together: the assembling of ourselves is where we stir each other up unto love and good works. God gave us the whole day so we can focus on growing in him and in each other. Not coming, sitting, and taking off -- actually communicating: that you want people in your life, that you want to grow together.

**Speaking the truth in love** (Ephesians 4:15). This is a how-to of discipleship. It's not "calling it as it is" -- it's speaking the truth in love for the specific purpose of helping the other person grow in Jesus Christ. The truth can be used to hurt; that is sin. Discipleship uses truth to build.

**Every member doing his or her part** (Ephesians 4:16): *"From whom the whole body, joined and knit together by what every joint supplies, according to the effective working by which every part does its share, causes growth."* There are no bench players. Everybody is in the game. Everybody is doing the work of discipleship. We only grow as our slowest-growing person grows.

Being filled with the Spirit (Ephesians 5:18-21) is not a mystical experience -- it is described by Paul in concrete actions: speaking to one another in the word, giving thanks, submitting to one another. We'll continue with specific practical suggestions next week.
